AppraisalOS — Property Insurance Appraisal Platform
Platform Documentation
How-to guides by role · Section reference · Procedural overview
HOW-TO GUIDE BY ROLE
U
Umpire / Neutral
Presiding Umpire
The umpire is a neutral, independent third party agreed upon by both appraisers. You preside over the panel hearing and are responsible for issuing the binding award.
Your responsibilities
01Open the matter and confirm panel composition under Parties & Panel.
02Review both parties' submitted briefs and evidence under Briefs and Evidence.
03Monitor the Disputed Issues matrix to understand the full scope of financial variance.
04Conduct the panel hearing under Hearings. Review each issue, record findings issue-by-issue, and mark items addressed.
05Enter determined award values in Awards, generate the draft award document, then execute with your signature.
06Any two of three panel signatures bind the award. Once bound, download the signed PDF from the Awards screen.
C
Carrier
Carrier Appraiser
You represent the insurer's position on the amount of loss. Your role is to prepare and submit a complete appraisal package and advocate your position before the panel.
Your responsibilities
01Begin at My Submission. Upload your appraisal brief, carrier estimate (Xactimate or equivalent), policy documentation, and inspection reports.
02Upload your estimate as a PDF. The platform's AI will extract line items into the Disputed Issues matrix automatically.
03Submit to panel. Your documents are delivered to the umpire immediately; your brief is shared with the PH appraiser 14 days before the hearing.
04Monitor the Disputed Issues matrix to compare your position against the policyholder's estimate once both are submitted.
05Attend the panel hearing under Hearings. Observe issue-by-issue review and panel communications in real time.
06Once the umpire issues the award, sign under Awards if you concur. Two signatures bind.
P
Policyholder
PH Appraiser
You represent the insured's position on the amount of loss. Your role is to document and advocate the full scope and fair value of the insured's loss.
Your responsibilities
01Begin at My Submission. Upload your appraisal brief, contractor estimate, proof of loss, photos, and supporting expert reports.
02Upload your estimate as a PDF. The AI reads the document and populates your side of the Disputed Issues matrix with extracted line items.
03Submit to panel. Your brief is released to the carrier appraiser 14 days before the hearing date.
04Review the Disputed Issues matrix to identify high-variance items and prepare your hearing position.
05Attend the panel hearing under Hearings. Monitor issue-by-issue deliberation and panel communications in real time.
06Once the umpire issues the award, sign under Awards if you concur. Two signatures bind the award.
SECTION REFERENCE — WHAT EACH TAB MEANS
⊞
Dashboard
The case overview screen. Displays the full docket header — matter number, insured property, policy number, date of loss, and appraisal status — alongside a financial positions summary showing the carrier position, policyholder position, total variance at issue, open items, and agreed items. Also contains the matter timeline and a category-level breakdown of disputed variance. This is the first screen you see after login and the primary orientation point for the case.
◉
Active Cases
The case register. Lists all appraisal matters currently under management on the platform. Each row displays the case number, insured property address, policy number, date of loss, current status, and the next required action. Click any case to navigate to its dashboard. In a multi-matter deployment, this screen functions as the primary landing point for case selection.
⊟
Notices
The formal notice register. Tracks all procedural notices in the appraisal proceeding — including the original appraisal demand letter (the document invoking the appraisal clause), appraiser appointment letters from each party, umpire confirmation notice, and hearing scheduling notices. All notices are indexed with date, issuing party, and filing status. This section provides the official record of how and when the appraisal was formally initiated and constituted.
⊠
Briefs
The appraisal brief index. Contains each party's formal appraisal submission — the written document presenting their position on the scope and amount of the loss. Carrier and policyholder briefs are displayed in separate indexed evidence tables with role-based access control: the umpire sees all documents immediately; each appraiser's brief becomes available to the other party 14 days before the scheduled hearing date. The submission completeness index confirms whether all required brief categories have been filed.
⊡
Evidence
The full evidence index. Contains all supporting documentation submitted by both parties beyond the formal brief — photographs, engineering and expert reports, inspection reports, policy documents, proof of loss statements, invoices, receipts, email exchanges, lease agreements, and case law references. Documents are indexed by party and category. The umpire has full access; party appraisers can view their own documents at all times, and gain access to the opposing party's documents when the umpire opens proceedings.
⊞
My Submission
The document filing portal for carrier and policyholder appraisers. This is where you upload your full appraisal package — organised into required categories including the appraisal notice, claim introduction, policy, proof of loss, estimates, expert reports, photographs, and more. The platform's AI automatically reads uploaded estimate PDFs and extracts line items directly into the Disputed Issues matrix. A deadline tracker shows days remaining until submission is due. Appraiser-only; umpires do not have a submission portal.
⊞
Disputed Issues
The automated dispute matrix. A line-item comparison of the carrier and policyholder positions, generated from the estimates each appraiser uploads. For each line item, the matrix shows: category, item description, carrier quantity/unit/total, policyholder quantity/unit/total, dollar variance, agreement status, and priority level. High-variance items are flagged. Items where both parties are within $200 are automatically agreed and awarded at the higher value. Clicking any row expands dispute notes and, for the umpire, provides a private working notes field. This is the quantitative core of the appraisal dispute.
◈
Hearings
The digital tribunal hearing room. A three-column interface: the issue docket lists all disputed items sorted by priority with their status; the centre panel shows issue-level detail including party positions, supporting evidence, and (for the umpire) a determination form for recording findings issue-by-issue; the right panel shows the panel composition with presence indicators, an accumulating decision record of all findings recorded during the session, and the secure panel communications log. The umpire records a finding (carrier / PH / split / agreed) and an award amount for each issue, building the decision record in real time.
◆
Awards
The binding award drafting and execution screen. A three-step workflow: (1) Enter determined award values for each disputed line item — pre-populated from hearing findings recorded by the umpire; (2) Review the formal award document, which includes full legal recitals, the award schedule, governing law provisions, binding effect clause, and all other standard appraisal award provisions; (3) Execute — panel members sign digitally. Any two of three signatures bind the award. The bound award can be downloaded as a signed PDF. Payment is subject to applicable deductibles and prior payments under the claim.
○
Parties & Panel
The case roster. Lists the two disputing parties (insured and insurer) and the three appointed panel members (carrier appraiser, policyholder appraiser, and neutral umpire) with their firms, roles, and confirmation status. The tripartite panel structure is fundamental to the appraisal process: each party appoints their own qualified appraiser, and the two appraisers either agree on an umpire or the umpire is appointed by a court. Any two of the three panel members may bind the award.
≡
Activity Log
The full audit trail. A time-stamped, immutable record of every action taken on the matter — document uploads, submission deliveries, AI extractions, matrix updates, finding recordings, signatures, and system events. All entries are attributed to the acting user and timestamped. The activity log serves as the procedural record of the appraisal proceeding and is available to all panel members. It cannot be edited or deleted.
APPRAISAL PROCESS — END TO END
01
Demand
Policyholder invokes the appraisal clause. Both parties appoint appraisers. Umpire is agreed upon or appointed.
02
Submission
Each appraiser uploads their complete appraisal package through My Submission. AI extracts estimate line items.
03
Analysis
The Disputed Issues matrix is generated automatically. Variances are calculated and prioritised by dollar amount.
04
Hearing
Panel convenes in the digital hearing room. Umpire reviews each issue, hears positions, and records findings.
05
Award
Umpire drafts the binding award. Any two panel members sign. Award is final and binding upon execution.
06
Payment
Insurer pays the award amount subject to applicable deductibles and prior payments under the claim.
NAAS — North American Appraisal System  ·  Platform Documentation  ·  All proceedings are time-stamped and archived
SECTION GUIDE
AppraisalOS — Property Insurance Appraisal Platform
Platform Overview
What NAAS is · How it works · Who it is built for
WHAT IS NAAS
Insurance Appraisal, Digitised
NAAS is a structured digital platform for managing insurance property appraisal disputes from demand through binding award. It replaces disorganised email chains, disconnected spreadsheets, and paper-based submissions with a single, role-aware case record system.
Built Around the Appraisal Clause
Every insurance policy containing an appraisal clause gives both the insured and insurer the right to demand appraisal when they disagree on the amount of loss. NAAS is purpose-built to manage that process — from the moment the clause is invoked to the moment the award is signed and delivered.
CORE CAPABILITIES
Case Management
Full case docket with matter number, property record, party roster, panel composition, timeline, and proceeding status. All in one structured record.
AI Estimate Extraction
Upload an estimate PDF. The platform reads it and automatically extracts every line item — categories, quantities, units, and totals — into a structured dispute matrix.
Dispute Matrix
Side-by-side comparison of carrier and policyholder positions on every disputed line item, with variance calculation, priority flagging, and auto-agreement logic.
Digital Hearing Room
A structured tribunal interface for the panel hearing — issue docket, position detail, evidence index, umpire determination form, and live decision record, all in one screen.
Binding Award Generation
Automated generation of the full formal award document — with recitals, line-item schedule, governing law provisions, and binding effect clause. Digital signatures from any two of three panel members bind the award.
Access Control & Audit Trail
Role-based document access enforced automatically. Time-stamped, immutable activity log of every action. Briefs released to opposing appraisers 14 days before the hearing.
WHO USES NAAS
Umpires
Neutral arbitrators managing the hearing, recording findings issue-by-issue, and issuing the binding award.
Carrier Appraisers
Insurer-appointed appraisers submitting and presenting the carrier's position on the scope and amount of the loss.
PH Appraisers
Policyholder-appointed appraisers documenting and advocating the full scope and fair value of the insured's loss.
Administrators
Platform operators and claim managers who create matters, configure panels, and oversee proceedings across a portfolio of cases.
JURISDICTION & COMPLIANCE
NAAS supports appraisal proceedings under United States and Canadian insurance law. Award documents are generated in accordance with applicable state or provincial insurance statutes and include governing law provisions specific to the jurisdiction of each matter. Electronic signatures are treated as valid and binding under applicable electronic commerce legislation. The platform is jurisdiction-agnostic at the proceeding level — each matter specifies its own governing jurisdiction at creation.
NAAS — North American Appraisal System  ·  Platform Overview

Support & Contact
Technical support · Procedural queries · Platform access
SUPPORT CHANNELS
Technical Support
[email protected]
Response within 4 hours
For issues with the platform — login problems, document upload failures, AI extraction errors, missing data, or any other technical difficulty. Include your matter number and a description of the issue.
Procedural Queries
[email protected]
Response within 1 business day
For questions about the appraisal process — submission deadlines, document access rules, hearing procedures, award execution, or jurisdiction-specific requirements. Note that NAAS does not provide legal advice; consult qualified counsel for legal questions.
Account & Access
[email protected]
Response within 1 business day
For issues with platform access — new matter setup, panel member invitations, role changes, account credentials, or firm-level administration. Include your matter number and the names and roles of the parties involved.
Enterprise & Licensing
[email protected]
Response within 2 business days
For insurance carriers, law firms, and appraisal firms interested in portfolio-level access, API integration, white-labelling, or volume licensing. Includes dedicated onboarding and a named account manager.
COMMON QUESTIONS
I uploaded an estimate but the dispute matrix is empty.
Ensure the file is a PDF and is under 32MB. The AI extraction runs automatically after upload — allow up to 30 seconds. If the extraction fails, an error message will appear in the upload category. Try re-uploading the file. If the issue persists, contact technical support with your matter number and the filename.
I cannot see the opposing party's brief.
Brief access for party appraisers opens 14 days before the scheduled hearing date. If the hearing date has not been set, access will remain restricted until it is. If the hearing date has passed and you still cannot see the brief, contact support.
The award will not let me sign.
Only panel members (carrier appraiser, policyholder appraiser, and umpire) may sign the award. Confirm you are logged in with the correct role. If your role is correct and the sign button does not appear, contact technical support.
I need to change a panel member after the matter was created.
Panel member changes after matter creation require administrator action. Contact [email protected] with your matter number, the name of the member being replaced, and the name and contact details of the replacement. Note that any documents already submitted will remain on record.
Is the platform admissible as a record of the proceeding?
The platform's activity log is a time-stamped, immutable record of all actions taken during the proceeding. Award documents generated by the platform include full legal provisions and are executable by digital signature under applicable electronic commerce legislation. Consult qualified legal counsel regarding admissibility in your specific jurisdiction.
